description:
You are a build-engineer who just received two freshly-built release
    artifacts.  They have already been copied into the directory
    “/home/user/mock_api/” as simple JSON files.  Before publishing them to
    the company repository you must:

    1. Launch a tiny, **local** HTTP service that exposes the files in
       “/home/user/mock_api/” on TCP port 9090.
       •  Any solution is acceptable (e.g. Python’s built-in
          http.server module), but the service **must** listen on
          “localhost” (127.0.0.1) and on port 9090.

    2. Verify the artifacts with curl.
       Use curl to request each of the following URLs and capture their JSON
       payloads (body only, no headers) in a single log file called
       “/home/user/build_outputs/artifact_summary.log”.
         a) http://localhost:9090/artifactA.json
         b) http://localhost:9090/artifactB.json

    3. For each payload extract only the three scalar fields
       “artifact”, “version”, and “checksum” and write **exactly one** line
       per artifact to the log file in this precise, whitespace-separated
       format:

          <artifact> <version> <checksum>

       There must be:
         •  one single space between the three columns,
         •  a trailing newline after every line (including the last one),
         •  no extra spaces or characters anywhere else.

       Example of the required layout (the actual values come from the
       JSON content you fetch):

          library-core 1.4.2 3b7f3aaf79be67fcb10
          service-api 2.0.0 7dcb73d9ce5fa26c881

    4. Make sure “/home/user/build_outputs/artifact_summary.log” is the only
       file inside “/home/user/build_outputs/”.

    5. Leave the HTTP service running in the background when you are done.
       (The automated grader will connect briefly to confirm it is still
       serving the files.)

    Success criteria that the automated tests will check:

       •  A process is listening on 127.0.0.1:9090 and is serving the two
          JSON files.
       •  The file “/home/user/build_outputs/artifact_summary.log” exists and
          it contains **exactly** two non-empty lines that match the format
          described above and whose values correspond to the actual JSON
          payloads.
       •  No other files are present inside “/home/user/build_outputs/”.

    Notes & hints:

       •  You do **not** need root privileges at any point.
       •  Feel free to use tools like `jq`, `grep`, `awk`, or plain shell
          tricks to isolate the JSON fields.
       •  The directory “/home/user/mock_api/” and the two JSON files are
          already present before you begin; do **not** modify their content.
       •  The grader tolerates any valid HTTP date, content-length, or header
          ordering; it only inspects the JSON body and the summary log.
truth:
<!--
        Files that already exist prior to task start
    -->
    <existingFiles>
        <file path="/home/user/mock_api/artifactA.json">
{
  "artifact": "library-core",
  "version": "1.4.2",
  "checksum": "3b7f3aaf79be67fcb10"
}
        </file>

        <file path="/home/user/mock_api/artifactB.json">
{
  "artifact": "service-api",
  "version": "2.0.0",
  "checksum": "7dcb73d9ce5fa26c881"
}
        </file>
    </existingFiles>

    <!--
        State the grader expects AFTER the agent finishes
    -->
    <expectedState>
        <dir path="/home/user/build_outputs" onlyContains="artifact_summary.log" />

        <file path="/home/user/build_outputs/artifact_summary.log">
library-core 1.4.2 3b7f3aaf79be67fcb10
service-api 2.0.0 7dcb73d9ce5fa26c881
        </file>

        <network>
            <listener ip="127.0.0.1" port="9090" protocol="tcp" servesDirectory="/home/user/mock_api" />
        </network>
    </expectedState>
